calendarNotes:
Miami-Dade County Public Schools begins the 2026-2027 school year on August 13, 2026 — earlier than most national peers outside of Texas. Spring Break runs March 22–26, 2027, and the school year ends June 3, 2027 — one of the latest last days of any large Florida district. Miami-Dade's later end date compared to most of the country reflects Florida's requirement for a minimum number of instructional hours and the district's use of teacher planning days distributed throughout the year rather than concentrated at the end.

In 2026-2027, the three South Florida counties — Miami-Dade, Broward, and Palm Beach — share the same Spring Break window (March 22–26), which is less common than it might appear. In many prior years, the three counties have staggered their breaks by a few days to a week. Families coordinating cross-county schedules should verify dates annually rather than assuming alignment. Orange County Public Schools (Orlando area) breaks a full week earlier, March 15–19. For South Florida hotel and beach pricing, the March 22–26 window is consistently one of the most expensive weeks of the year across all three counties.

Miami-Dade sits in the Atlantic hurricane zone, and Florida law allows school districts to bank up to three makeup days into the calendar year. If extended school closures due to a hurricane exceed this buffer, the district may be required to add days to the end of the school year, potentially pushing the last day into mid-June. Families booking late-May or early-June travel from Miami should use refundable options or wait until February to confirm the end-of-year schedule. Hurricane season runs June 1 through November 30, but the most active period for South Florida is August through October — overlapping directly with the school year.

M-DCPS publishes its official school calendar in English, Spanish, and Haitian Creole — reflecting the district's position as one of the most linguistically diverse public school systems in the country. Spanish is spoken at home by a large proportion of the student body, and the district operates numerous bilingual and dual-language programs. Haitian Creole-speaking families represent one of the largest non-Spanish-speaking groups in the district. The calendar is available in all three languages on the official district website.
